claim: New transmission capacity does not wait on new generation: it fills in sequence — (1) currently-curtailed existing generation, immediately; (2) queue projects whose binding constraint is connection; (3) new build. Reconductoring is the cork on generation that already exists. Counter-view: curtailment is concentrated in specific regions (West Texas, California) so reconductoring elsewhere does not tap it; some curtailment is oversupply not congestion, which wires alone do not fix; and storage competes with wires as the curtailment fix
verifier: OPEN-ARGUMENT; builds on C20/C21; counter-view stated at equal prominence in §03 Act I

claim: The long-lived hazard in spent fuel is the transuranics created in fission (Pu-239 half-life ~24,000 yr), not the uranium itself; untreated spent fuel takes on the order of 100,000+ years to decay to the radiotoxicity of the ore it came from, whereas a fast-reactor closed fuel cycle reduces the residual to a custody time of roughly 300–500 years — fast-spectrum neutrons can fission transuranics, thermal-spectrum ones mostly cannot (IAEA: ~30 g per kg needing 200–300 yr; peer-reviewed equilibrium ~500 yr)
verifier: IAEA Bulletin + Progress in Nuclear Energy actinide-recycling literature — audited (physics)

claim: The waste-lifetime benefit follows the neutron SPECTRUM, not the coolant/fuel-form: salt-cooled THERMAL designs (Kairos KP-FHR, NuScale VOYGR, GE-Hitachi BWRX-300, X-energy Xe-100) get no waste-lifetime benefit; the fast-spectrum US near-term designs are Oklo Aurora (15 MW, sodium, INL, ~2028) and TerraPower Natrium (345 MWe, sodium, Kemmerer WY, ~2030); fast molten-salt designs (TerraPower MCFR, Moltex SSR-W) sit further out
verifier: World Nuclear News + vendor design documentation — descriptive design facts

claim: A fast-vs-thermal cost premium is expected via HALEU fuel (~19% enriched, ~2.5–3x natural-U per kWh, currently scarce), sodium handling, and longer Gen-IV licensing, but its MAGNITUDE is not yet demonstrated: Natrium FOAK ~$4B/345 MW ≈ ~$11.6k/kW (roughly half DOE-funded) while the cancelled NuScale THERMAL project reached ~$20k/kW, so FOAK noise swamps the spectrum effect; FOAK SMRs generally land ~$80–150/MWh with ~$50–80/MWh NOAK targets
verifier: signpost: a fast-vs-thermal $/MWh premium is isolated from FOAK noise once multiple US SMRs (fast and thermal) reach NOAK

claim: Because the US never opened a permanent repository, taxpayers pay utilities up to ~$800M/yr in damages for on-site spent-fuel storage — ~$11.1B paid since 1998, a liability the DOE Inspector General projects reaching ~$44.5B; 95,000+ metric tons of spent fuel are stranded at 79 sites in 39 states; ~$15B was sunk into Yucca Mountain and ~$37B sits unused in the ratepayer-funded Nuclear Waste Fund — a multi-tens-of-billions liability that is the cost of POLITICAL failure to open any repository, not an intrinsic per-year price of long half-lives
verifier: GAO-21-603; CRS RL33461; DOE Inspector General audit (CNBC reporting) — audited

claim: OPEN-ARGUMENT: a fractional fast-spectrum premium buys a roughly two-to-three-order-of-magnitude reduction in waste custody time against a status quo already costing tens of billions, so a total-system comparison (reactor premium + reprocessing vs avoided storage liability) is the right frame and DESERVES TO BE RUN — posed here, not settled. Counter-view (equal prominence): a closed cycle still needs a repository (shorter custody, same facility; cost driven by heat load, volume, and politics more than raw half-life); major fuel-cycle economics studies and French practice find closed cycles net MORE expensive than once-through after crediting disposal savings; disposal is a small share of nuclear LCOE (historical US fee ~1 mill/kWh ≈ ~$1/MWh), so the avoided-cost pool is small, while the $44.5B liability is a political-failure cost a fast fleet does not automatically retire
verifier: OPEN-ARGUMENT; builds on C23/C25/C26; total-system frame posed not settled; counter-view (MIT Future of the Nuclear Fuel Cycle 2011) at equal prominence

claim: OPEN-ARGUMENT: the solar + battery + nuclear-floor architecture is where the cost curves point — solar and batteries take the diurnal cycle, nuclear and geothermal run flat as a ~15–20% clean-firm floor covering the winter wall, and batteries (not reactors) do the fast balancing; Natrium's built-in thermal storage is the industry building for exactly this. Counter-view (equal prominence): extrapolated learning curves can break (materials, tariffs, interconnection); demand could overshoot all supply math (the LBNL 649-TWh case); and the US may simply not un-tariff the Chinese cost curve, in which case the gas bridge extends instead
verifier: OPEN-ARGUMENT; builds on C28–C32; counter-view at equal prominence

With C28 (US pays a ~44% battery premium on a ~3 GW cell base), the US is a CONSUMER of waves 1 and 2 — paying a maker's premium without a maker's industry

claim: The advanced-nuclear first-deployment race is decided and the US lost it: China's Linglong One (125 MWe) enters commercial service H1 2026 as the world's first land-based commercial SMR, built in 58 months; China's HTR-PM has been commercial since 2023 and Russia's floating SMR since 2020; the US has zero operating SMRs. US strengths are real: the largest advanced-design portfolio (incl. the fast-spectrum designs of C19/C24), deepest capital (more than $10B federal since 2020; X-energy $700M round), strongest demand pull (data centers), and HALEU onshoring underway — but Russia dominates HALEU production, Centrus is building the first US cascade at Piketon targeting 6 MTU/yr within 42 months, and more than half of HALEU-dependent SMR projects lacked binding fuel supply as of early 2025
verifier: smrintel 2026; WNN / CNNC reporting; Centrus disclosures — audited (near-resolved signpost: Linglong One commercial operation H1 2026)

claim: OPEN-ARGUMENT (the maker thesis): the lesson of waves 1–2 is that whoever owns the factory owns the learning curve, so the contest still winnable is not FIRST SMR (lost) but the NEXT-WAVE manufacturing ecosystem — fast-spectrum reactors, HALEU enrichment, fuel recycling, factory fabrication — which no nation yet owns; on this argument a deliberate public investment across that chain (national-security framing) is how the US BECOMES the maker of the next wave rather than merely remaining a consumer. Counter-view (equal prominence): industrial policy has a picking-winners failure mode (NuScale precedent); China's execution tempo plus its IAEA-certified export machine (Belt and Road) may capture the market regardless of US design leadership; and the consumer position has real consumer-surplus value IF tariffs do not destroy it — note the status quo (consumer AND premium) is the one position with no defenders
verifier: OPEN-ARGUMENT; builds on C28/C34/C35; signposts: Centrus Piketon delivering ≥6 MTU/yr HALEU by 2030; cumulative US SMR order book ≥5 GW by 2030; counter-view at equal prominence

claim: The advanced-nuclear mineral chokepoint map. Rare-earth exposure of SMRs is MARGINAL: magnets appear only in pumps, drives, and compact generators, so a 100-GW fleet needs ~2,000–5,000 t of rare-earth magnets — a rounding error against China's ~300,000 t/yr magnet output. Contrast: robotics rides on NdFeB permanent magnets (~90% China-controlled refining/manufacture, export controls active); reactors do not. The REAL chokepoints, ranked: (1) HALEU — Russia sole commercial supplier as of 2024, a 100-GW fleet needs 500–1,000 t/yr = 50–100x current Western capacity, US onshoring underway (cross-ref C35/Centrus); (2) hafnium — thinnest non-fuel input, 100-GW fleet needs 5–10x current global production, supply inelastic as a byproduct of Zr refining, France ~half of global supply (ally); (3) nuclear-grade zirconium — ore abundant (Australia/South Africa) but nuclear-grade processing concentrated in a few facilities (China, US, France), 100-GW fleet needs 3–4x current nuclear-grade output; (4) nuclear graphite — the one genuine China exposure, and only for high-temperature designs (Kairos, X-energy); (5) beryllium — the US produces ~half of world supply, so for salt designs the leverage runs TOWARD the US
verifier: Payne Institute 2026; CATF advanced-nuclear supply chains 2025; criticalstrategicmetals / USGS-derived production shares; ScienceDirect minerals-geopolitics 2026 — audited

claim: The clock on the mineral bill: building SMR-scale mineral and fuel supply chains is estimated at 10–15 years, so the buildout would have to begin roughly now to serve 2035–2040 fleets; hafnium at scale is expected to stay tight even with France onside; and China is building HALEU conversion/enrichment capacity while explicitly modelling its rare-earths playbook for uranium — so the fuel window would close on the same clock as the reactor window
verifier: signpost: US/allied HALEU capacity ≥25 t/yr by 2030 — a FLEET-SCALE aggregate that nests above (does not duplicate) the single-facility C35/C36 signpost of Centrus Piketon ≥6 MTU/yr by 2030
